summary: Matched the MSL wchar_io translation unit by replacing asm/MSL/wchar_io.s with src/MSL/wchar_io.c and adding a companion src/MSL/wchar_io.h. The matched function is fwide, implemented against a reconstructed FILE layout and __file_modes bitfield so the previous assembly logic for file kind and stream orientation could compile from C. obj_files.mk was updated to build src/MSL/wchar_io.o instead of asm/MSL/wchar_io.o.
what_changed: Deleted asm/MSL/wchar_io.s, which contained the hand-written assembly for fwide at address range 80326098-80326114.
Added src/MSL/wchar_io.c with a C implementation of int fwide(FILE* stream, int mode).

Added src/MSL/wchar_io.h defining enum __file_kinds, enum __file_orientation, typedef struct _FILE FILE, typedef unsigned short wchar_t, __file_modes bitfields, and the _FILE struct layout needed by fwide.
Changed obj_files.mk so TEXT_O_FILES uses $(BUILD_DIR)/src/MSL/wchar_io.o instead of $(BUILD_DIR)/asm/MSL/wchar_io.o.

The C fwide logic returns 0 for __closed_file, reads stream->mode.file_orientation, sets orientation from __unoriented based on positive or negative mode, returns mode for unoriented streams, returns 1 for __wide_oriented, and returns -1 for __char_oriented.
smart_moves: Reconstructed the FILE mode field as named bitfields, allowing assembly operations on offsets 4 and 5 to become readable C field accesses such as stream->mode.file_kind and stream->mode.file_orientation.
Introduced explicit enum names for file kinds and orientations instead of preserving raw constants, making the fwide switch match the library semantics while remaining compact.
Kept the matched C implementation minimal: fwide only depends on the subset of FILE and mode metadata needed for the observed assembly behavior.
Moved the object from asm to src in obj_files.mk in the same change, completing the asm-to-C replacement cleanly.
decomp_lessons: For MSL library code, matching small functions can require reconstructing shared runtime structs first; here fwide depended on FILE and its packed mode bitfields.

Bitfield order and enum values matter for matching. The assembly used bit extraction and insertion around the FILE mode bytes, so the __file_modes layout had to expose file_kind and file_orientation at the correct positions.
When a function only inspects a few logical fields, a header can still need a broader struct layout to preserve offsets for those fields.
Replacing an asm object with a src object requires updating obj_files.mk so the build system stops assembling the old file and compiles the new translation unit.
naming_conventions: MSL internal enum constants use double-underscore names such as __closed_file, __disk_file, __console_file, __unavailable_file, __unoriented, __char_oriented, and __wide_oriented.
The FILE type is declared via typedef struct _FILE FILE, with the backing struct named struct _FILE.
The file mode bitfield type is named __file_modes and contains snake_case field names such as open_mode, io_mode, buffer_mode, file_kind, file_orientation, and binary_io.
The header uses an all-caps include guard _WCHAR_IO_H_.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Converted the initial lhz/rlwinm check on stream offset 4 into stream->mode.file_kind == __closed_file.
Converted the lbz/rlwinm extraction from offset 5 into stream->mode.file_orientation.
Converted the rlwimi writes that set orientation bits into assignments to stream->mode.file_orientation.
Used a switch over __file_orientation to reproduce the assembly branch table-like compare flow for unoriented, wide-oriented, and char-oriented cases.
Preserved fwide return behavior from the assembly: 0 for closed file, mode for previously unoriented streams, 1 for wide-oriented, and -1 for char-oriented.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that reuse or modify src/MSL/wchar_io.h or the FILE struct layout.
Check whether __file_modes bitfield ordering was later shared with other MSL stdio headers.
Search for additional MSL functions depending on file_orientation or fwide semantics.
Look for later cleanup replacing local wchar_t or FILE definitions with common MSL headers.
